questions.question: A company wants to minimize its PCI-DSS compliance scope for handling credit card payments. What is the most effective architectural approach?
options: Store all credit card data encrypted with AES-256 — encryption brings you out of scope.

Implement tokenization — use a payment processor (Stripe, Adyen) that handles all actual card data and returns a token. Your application only ever sees the token, never the real card number, CVV, or expiry date. This reduces your PCI-DSS scope from your entire payment stack to almost nothing.
Use HTTPS instead of HTTP for all payment pages to achieve PCI compliance.
Store CVV codes in a separate database from card numbers to reduce risk.
questions.answer: Implement tokenization — use a payment processor (Stripe, Adyen) that handles all actual card data and returns a token. Your application only ever sees the token, never the real card number, CVV, or expiry date. This reduces your PCI-DSS scope from your entire payment stack to almost nothing.
questions.explanation: Note: PCI-DSS explicitly prohibits storing CVV after authorization regardless of encryption. Tokenization means you don't store card data at all. A PCI audit of a tokenized system typically takes days instead of months.

questions.question: Under GDPR, a company discovers a breach of personal data at 3 PM on Monday. By what time must they notify the supervisory authority, and what should be documented in the notification?
options: Within 30 days — GDPR gives companies time to fully investigate before reporting.

Within 72 hours of discovery (by 3 PM Thursday at the latest). The notification must include: the nature of the breach, categories and approximate number of individuals affected, likely consequences of the breach, and measures taken or proposed to address it — even if the full investigation is not yet complete.
Within 24 hours for breaches affecting more than 1,000 users.
Only if financial data was breached — GDPR does not require breach notification for non-financial personal data.
questions.answer: Within 72 hours of discovery (by 3 PM Thursday at the latest). The notification must include: the nature of the breach, categories and approximate number of individuals affected, likely consequences of the breach, and measures taken or proposed to address it — even if the full investigation is not yet complete.
questions.explanation: This 72-hour clock is why pre-built incident response runbooks are critical. An organization that discovers a breach on a Saturday morning and has no runbook will almost certainly miss the 72-hour notification deadline.

questions.question: What does ISO 27001 certification actually prove about an organization, and why is it valuable for B2B enterprise sales?
options: ISO 27001 proves the organization has never had a security breach.

ISO 27001 certification proves that an accredited, independent auditor has verified the organization has implemented a documented Information Security Management System (ISMS) with systematic risk assessment, appropriate controls, and continuous improvement processes — not that it's perfectly secure, but that it manages security systematically.
ISO 27001 means all software passes SAST and DAST scanning.
ISO 27001 certification eliminates the need for PCI-DSS compliance for payment processing.
questions.answer: ISO 27001 certification proves that an accredited, independent auditor has verified the organization has implemented a documented Information Security Management System (ISMS) with systematic risk assessment, appropriate controls, and continuous improvement processes — not that it's perfectly secure, but that it manages security systematically.
questions.explanation: Enterprise buyers (banks, hospitals, government agencies) require ISO 27001 certification from their SaaS vendors before signing contracts. It's a trust signal that removes your company from the 'too risky to buy from' category.
